sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Ankle and foot presentations are common in site, sporting, and slip-trip-fall incidents. Most are sprains or load-related injuries, but fracture, syndesmosis injury, Achilles rupture, or infection should not be missed.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Lateral or medial ankle pain
- Swelling, bruising, limp, or inability to run or hop
- Midfoot pain, plantar pain, or heel pain
- Giving way, instability, or pain with push-off

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Use the Ottawa ankle or foot rules when the patient fits the rule and the question is fracture risk after acute trauma
- Escalate for gross deformity, Achilles rupture, open injury, syndesmosis concern, neurovascular compromise, or inability to weight-bear with fracture suspicion
- Early graded loading is usually preferable to prolonged complete rest once fracture is excluded

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen weight-bearing, bony tenderness, tendon integrity, and neurovascular status
- Support the limb, advise relative rest, compression, elevation, and simple pain relief within scope
- Escalate for suspected fracture, tendon rupture, or rapidly worsening swelling

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Differentiate lateral sprain, syndesmosis injury, Achilles injury, and foot stress or bony pain patterns
- Begin early range, calf activation, and graded reloading when safe
- Add balance and proprioception work in recovery planning to reduce recurrence
- Refer for imaging or specialist review when Ottawa-rule positive, tendon rupture suspected, or symptoms persist beyond expected recovery

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Recurrent ankle sprain should trigger review of balance, calf strength, footwear, and worksite terrain exposure
